title: W11: VAEs, Generative Vision Models, and Late Topics
blurb: High priority inside the vision block. VAE likelihood, ELBO, and reparameterization appear in 2024, 2025, and 2026.

knowledge_points.title: VAE Likelihood and Intractability
knowledge_points.recap: VAEs introduce a latent random variable z with a prior $p(z)$ and decoder likelihood $p_\theta(x\mid z)$. Direct marginal likelihood requires an integral over latent space.

questions.q: Write the VAE data likelihood $p_\theta(x)$ and explain why it cannot be optimized directly.
questions.answer: $p_\theta(x)=\int p(z)p_\theta(x\mid z)\,dz$. Here $p(z)$ is the latent prior, often $N(0,I)$, and $p_\theta(x\mid z)$ is the decoder likelihood. The integral over all latent vectors is generally intractable in high-dimensional latent space, so direct maximum likelihood is not feasible.

questions.q: Why are standard deterministic autoencoders not used directly for image generation?
questions.answer: A standard autoencoder learns deterministic mappings for reconstruction but does not define a known, well-structured latent distribution to sample from. A VAE imposes a prior $p(z)$, samples z from that prior, and decodes through $p_\theta(x\mid z)$ to generate images.

knowledge_points.title: ELBO and Reparameterization Trick
knowledge_points.recap: Training VAEs uses the ELBO: a reconstruction te$rm$ minus a KL te$rm$. Reparameterization makes sampling differentiable with respect to encoder parameters.

questions.q: In the VAE ELBO, why is the expectation intractable and how is it approximated?
questions.answer: The expectation over z requires marginalizing over the latent space and evaluating the decoder for possible latent vectors. During training it is approximated by Monte Carlo sampling z from $q_\phi(z\mid x)$.

questions.q: How does the reparameterization trick allow backpropagation through the encoder?
questions.answer: The encoder outputs Gaussian parameters $\mu_\phi(x)$ and $\sigma_\phi(x)$. Instead of sampling z directly from $N(\mu,\sigma^2 I)$, sample $\epsilon$ from $N(0,I)$ and compute $z=\mu_\phi(x)+\sigma_\phi(x)\epsilon$. The randomness is isolated in $\epsilon$, so gradients can flow through $\mu_\phi$ and $\sigma_\phi$.
questions.extend: This exact explanation is required for full credit in the 2025 vision question.

questions.q: For a transposed convolution with input size 2 x 2, kernel size 3, stride 2, and padding 1, what is the output spatial size?
questions.answer: The output is 4 x 4, using the convention from the exam statement/solution.
